title: Brain Coverings
subtitle: Explore the skull, meninges and the space surrounding the brain.

cards.frontPrompt: What protects and surrounds the brain?
backPoints: The brain is protected by the skull and three meningeal layers.
The meninges are called the dura mater, arachnoid mater and pia mater.
These layers surround the brain and support the spaces and blood vessels around it.
We will work through them from outside to inside.

cards.frontPrompt: Which structure provides the brain's rigid outer protection?
backPoints: The skull forms a strong protective case around the brain.
It helps protect the brain from external injury.
Unlike other tissues, the skull cannot expand when pressure inside it rises.
The meninges lie between the skull and the brain.
cards.memoryLine: The skull protects the brain but cannot expand.

cards.frontPrompt: Which tough membrane lies beneath the skull?
backPoints: The dura mater is the tough outermost meningeal covering.
It lies immediately inside the skull.
It is thick, strong and fibrous.
Its strength helps protect and support the structures within the skull.
cards.memoryLine: Dura is the tough outer meningeal layer.

cards.frontPrompt: What lies beneath the dura and surrounds the CSF and cerebral arteries?
backPoints: The arachnoid mater is a thin membrane lying beneath the dura.
The subarachnoid space lies beneath the arachnoid and above the pia.
This space contains cerebrospinal fluid, commonly called CSF.
Major cerebral arteries also travel through this space.
cards.memoryLine: The subarachnoid space contains CSF and arteries.

cards.frontPrompt: Which meningeal layer closely hugs the surface of the brain?
backPoints: The pia mater is the delicate innermost meningeal layer.
It adheres closely to the surface of the brain.
It follows the brain into its sulci and around its gyri.
The subarachnoid space lies between the arachnoid and pia.
cards.memoryLine: Pia closely follows every contour of the brain.

cards.frontPrompt: Why is the subarachnoid space clinically important?
cards.backHeading: A Space Containing CSF and Arteries
backPoints: The subarachnoid space normally contains CSF.
Cerebral arteries travel through the same space.
If one of these arteries bleeds, blood can spread into the CSF-filled subarachnoid space.
This is why the anatomy of this space is important when learning about subarachnoid haemorrhage.
cards.memoryLine: SAH occurs where cerebral arteries travel through CSF.

cards.frontPrompt: Can you remember the protective layers from outside to inside?
backPoints: First comes the skull.
Beneath the skull lies the dura mater.
Next comes the arachnoid mater and the subarachnoid space.
The pia mater then closely covers the brain.
cards.memoryLine: Skull → dura → arachnoid → subarachnoid space → pia → brain.

cards.title: Identify the Meningeal Layers
cards.instruction: Select each meningeal layer from outside to inside.

steps.success: Correct! The dura mater is the tough outer layer.

steps.prompt: Now select the middle meningeal layer.
steps.success: Correct! The arachnoid lies beneath the dura.

steps.prompt: Finally select the layer attached directly to the brain.
steps.success: Correct! The pia mater closely follows the brain surface.

backPoints: Dura mater is the tough outer protective layer.
Arachnoid mater is the middle layer.
The subarachnoid space contains CSF and cerebral blood vessels.
Pia mater is tightly adherent to the brain surface.

quiz.question: Which structure provides rigid outer protection for the brain?
Which is the tough outermost meningeal layer?
Which meningeal layer lies directly beneath the dura?
Where is the subarachnoid space located?
What does the subarachnoid space normally contain?
Which layer closely follows the sulci and gyri of the brain?
Why can blood spread through the subarachnoid space during a subarachnoid haemorrhage?
options: Cerebral arteries travel through the CSF-filled space

quiz.question: Which sequence is correct from outside to inside?
